executiveSummary:
CosTERRA is an exceptionally well-crafted proposal that has matured significantly across multiple revision cycles. It presents a compelling case for developing a domain-specific generative AI model for Ukrainian construction cost estimation, grounded in a clearly articulated business problem, a unique proprietary dataset, and a well-justified need for a new model rather than adaptation of existing ones. The proposal demonstrates strong alignment with FFplus Call-2-Type-2 objectives across all three evaluation criteria, with particular strength in its thorough justification of why general-purpose LLMs fail for this task (backed by concrete experimental evidence) and its detailed ML lifecycle action plan.

The proposal addresses virtually all issues raised in prior review rounds: Ukraine's Digital Europe Programme eligibility is now explicitly cited with a source; the budget breakdown is detailed and clearly tabulated; AI/ML competence is better evidenced through named staff with specific publications and relevant project experience; the expert evaluation panel sub-contracting concern has been resolved by embedding expert evaluation within ALD's senior cost engineers; and mandatory eligibility declarations are now explicit. The structured-data encoder innovation adds genuine technical novelty beyond standard fine-tuning, and the full-parameter fine-tuning choice is now justified against LoRA/QLoRA with preliminary experimental evidence.

The remaining weaknesses are minor: the page count is likely tight (estimated 12–13 pages), some aspects of ZNU's deep learning track record could be further strengthened with specific publication titles, and the regulatory acceptance pathway for AI-generated construction documents in the Ukrainian legal context deserves a brief mention. These are refinements rather than structural deficiencies, and the proposal comfortably exceeds all scoring thresholds.

sectionScores.feedback: The Impact case is outstanding. The proposal presents a clearly quantified business problem (2,500 documents/year, 10,000+ person-days of specialist effort, 5–12% error rates) and translates this into concrete value propositions: €1.5M annual labour cost savings, SaaS revenue expansion with a €200–500/month willingness-to-pay across 3,000+ addressable customers in Ukraine alone, and a clear regional expansion pathway to post-Soviet markets with structurally similar building standards. The societal relevance is compelling — linking to Ukraine's post-conflict reconstruction needs and citing the World Bank assessment adds strategic weight. The vision of success is specific and credible: a production-ready model integrated into ALD's existing SaaS platform within 12 months of study completion. Alignment with FFplus call objectives is explicitly and systematically mapped across all six objectives. The exploitation plan is realistic because it builds on existing commercial infrastructure (the SaaS platform already exists and serves paying customers). Innovation prospects are strong: the structured-data encoder conditioning text generation on ERP database inputs represents a genuinely novel approach for the construction domain. The only minor gap is the absence of any discussion of regulatory acceptance of AI-generated cost estimation documents under Ukrainian construction law — while not critical for the innovation study scope, it would strengthen the exploitation narrative.

Conceptual and technical excellence are both strong. The conceptual framework is coherent: the problem is well-defined as conditional text generation with structured-data inputs, and the gap analysis against existing LLMs is thorough and evidence-based (0% compliance in GPT-4/Llama-3 testing, 78% hallucination rate on standard numbers, <15% numerical consistency). The justification for full-parameter fine-tuning over LoRA/QLoRA is now properly substantiated with preliminary experimental evidence (52% compliance vs. 85% target), directly addressing a concern from earlier reviews. The structured-data encoder is a well-motivated architectural innovation with appropriate literature backing. Model characteristics are well-specified: architecture (decoder-only transformer), size range (7–13B), candidate base models (Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B), hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, sequence length, precision, optimizer), and distributed training strategy (FSDP). Performance metrics are comprehensive with six quantitative targets and four baselines — the inclusion of both automated metrics (ROUGE-L, BERTScore, numerical consistency) and human evaluation (expert acceptance rate) is appropriate. Reproducibility is well-planned with MLflow, DVC, Git versioning, and seed fixing. Two minor gaps prevent a full 5: (1) the selection criteria for choosing among the three candidate base models in Month 2 could be more precisely defined (what specific benchmarks will determine the choice?); (2) the structured-data encoder architecture could benefit from slightly more technical detail on the embedding concatenation mechanism and how gradient flow through this component is managed during full-parameter fine-tuning.

The workplan is well-structured with clear task decomposition, temporal sequencing, and appropriate overlap between tasks. The Work Plan Table includes all required elements: tasks, durations, participants with effort, deliverables with due dates, technical descriptions, and computational resources per task. The 31 person-month total effort is realistic for the scope. The Month 7 pre-final report is explicitly planned within Task 4 with specific content commitments. The Data Management Plan addresses all FAIR principles with appropriate detail, including data formats, licensing (Apache 2.0 for code), retention policies, and data protection measures. The cost breakdown is detailed and compliant: ALD receives 68.5% of funding (above 50% threshold), both participants are within individual caps (€185K < €200K, €85K < €150K), total is €270K < €300K, no indirect costs or sub-contracting. HPC justification is thorough with a bottom-up calculation (1,800 hours × 3 runs + hyperparameter search + baselines + evaluation + contingency = 8,000 GPU-node-hours), clearly exceeding playground access. Consortium complementarity is demonstrated with a clear capability matrix. ZNU's role is strictly technical as required. Personnel rates are reasonable for Ukrainian market. Areas for minor improvement: (1) ZNU's AI/ML track record, while improved from earlier versions, would benefit from citing 2–3 specific publication titles rather than just counts and general topics — this is particularly important since the call requires 'proficiency in generative AI' to be documented; (2) the commercial HPC fallback plan (Lambda Cloud, CoreWeave at €15K) may be insufficient if EuroHPC access is significantly delayed — a brief contingency timeline would strengthen this; (3) the travel budget rationale could specify which FFplus events are anticipated.

strengths: Exceptionally well-quantified business problem with concrete metrics (2,500 docs/year, 10,000+ person-days, 5–12% error rates, €1.5M savings potential)
Compelling and evidence-based justification for why a new model is needed, including specific experimental results showing 0% compliance from existing LLMs and 52% from LoRA approaches
Unique proprietary dataset of sufficient scale (12,000 documents, 180,000 line items, 2.1M price records) with demonstrated availability and clear data preparation pipeline
Technically novel structured-data encoder architecture that goes beyond standard fine-tuning to address the specific challenge of conditioning on real-time ERP data
Comprehensive evaluation framework with six quantitative metrics, four baselines, and both automated and human evaluation methods
Strong consortium complementarity with no unnecessary overlap — ALD provides domain/data/software, ZNU provides AI/HPC expertise
Well-justified HPC resource request with bottom-up calculation significantly exceeding playground access
Thorough trustworthy AI risk assessment with specific, actionable mitigations for each identified risk
Clear exploitation pathway leveraging existing commercial SaaS infrastructure with quantified market sizing
All prior review concerns systematically addressed: eligibility documentation, budget breakdown, AI competence evidence, eligibility declarations, LoRA/QLoRA justification
Strong societal relevance linking to Ukraine's reconstruction needs and EU digital sovereignty objectives
Robust reproducibility plan using industry-standard tools (MLflow, DVC, Git)

weaknesses.issue: Base model selection criteria not fully specified — three candidates named but the decision protocol for Month 2 selection is vague
weaknesses.suggestion: Add a sentence specifying the benchmarks that will determine base model selection (e.g., 'Selection based on: (1) Ukrainian language perplexity on held-out construction text, (2) zero-shot numerical reasoning accuracy, (3) training throughput on target EuroHPC GPU architecture')

weaknesses.issue: Structured-data encoder architecture description lacks detail on embedding dimension matching, gradient flow management, and training stability considerations when concatenating heterogeneous input types
weaknesses.suggestion: Add 2–3 sentences describing how structured embeddings are projected to match transformer hidden dimensions and whether the encoder is trained jointly or pre-trained separately

weaknesses.issue: ZNU's generative AI proficiency is documented through publication counts and general topics but lacks specific evidence of LLM training or fine-tuning experience
weaknesses.suggestion: Cite 2–3 specific publication titles from Dr. Shylo and Dr. Petrov that are most relevant to transformer training and distributed deep learning, and mention any specific models they have trained at scale

weaknesses.issue: No discussion of regulatory acceptance of AI-generated construction cost estimation documents under Ukrainian law
weaknesses.suggestion: Add a brief statement clarifying that the human-in-the-loop review protocol ensures all AI-generated documents are reviewed and signed by certified cost engineers, maintaining legal validity under existing Ukrainian construction regulations

weaknesses.issue: Commercial HPC fallback budget (€15K) may be insufficient for meaningful training if EuroHPC access is significantly delayed
weaknesses.suggestion: Add a contingency timeline: 'If EuroHPC access is not confirmed by Month 3, commercial cloud resources will be scaled to €15K for initial training runs; full training will be rescheduled upon EuroHPC confirmation within the 10-month window'

improvementPriorities.recommendation: Add explicit selection criteria (Ukrainian perplexity, numerical reasoning, training throughput) for the Month 2 base model decision among Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, and Qwen2.5-7B
Replace generic publication counts with 2–3 specific, named publications from assigned staff demonstrating hands-on experience with transformer model training and distributed deep learning
Add one sentence confirming that certified human review maintains legal validity of AI-assisted documents under Ukrainian construction regulations
Add brief technical details on embedding projection, joint training strategy, and training stability measures for the structured-data encoder component
Specify a decision point (e.g., Month 3) for activating commercial HPC fallback and describe how this affects the training schedule

comments: Excellent summary that covers all essential elements: business problem, dataset, technical approach, consortium, expected outcomes, HPC requirements, and eligibility confirmations.
The explicit inclusion of eligibility declarations (non-FFplus beneficiary, non-Type-1 participation, Ukraine's Digital Europe Programme association) addresses prior review feedback effectively.
The summary clearly positions the study within the ML lifecycle scope (data preparation, model development, evaluation) without overstepping into deployment.
Well-calibrated length — provides sufficient detail for first-stage evaluation without redundancy.
Section 2 (Business Problem) is exceptionally well-quantified — the 2,500 documents/year, 4 person-days average, 10,000+ person-days total, and 5–12% error rate create a compelling case.

Section 4 (Why a New Model) is the strongest part of the proposal — the five-point argument against existing LLMs is systematic and evidence-based, including specific experimental results (0% compliance, 78% hallucination rate, 52% LoRA compliance).
The LoRA/QLoRA justification (Section 4, final paragraph) directly addresses the prior review concern and provides credible preliminary evidence for full fine-tuning necessity.
Section 5 (Expected Business Impact) provides credible, quantified projections with clear methodology — €1.5M savings, market sizing of 3,000 companies in Ukraine, €200–500/month WTP.
The regional expansion pathway (Kazakhstan, Georgia, Moldova) with structural similarity of building standards is a realistic medium-term exploitation plan.

Minor gap: no discussion of regulatory acceptance of AI-generated documents under Ukrainian construction law. While the human-in-the-loop protocol mitigates this, an explicit statement would strengthen the exploitation case.
Section 7 (Call Alignment) systematically maps to all six FFplus objectives — thorough and convincing.
The five objectives (O1–O5) are specific, measurable, and clearly mapped to the ML lifecycle phases.

Dataset description (Section 2) is comprehensive with specific numbers: 12,000 documents, 180,000 line items, 45,000 BOQ entries, 2.1M price records, 800 project templates. The four-step data preparation pipeline is well-defined.

Model characteristics (Section 3) include all required elements: architecture type, candidate base models, size range, hyperparameters, precision, optimizer, and distributed training strategy. The structured-data encoder is a genuine technical contribution.

Performance metrics table (Section 4) is exemplary: six quantitative metrics with specific targets and measurement methods, plus four baselines covering zero-shot, few-shot, LoRA, and current production system.

Trustworthy AI risk assessment (Section 5) identifies six specific risks with severity ratings and concrete mitigations. The hallucinated standards and numerical errors risks are correctly rated as high severity with actionable mitigations (constrained decoding, arithmetic verification layer).

Data Management Plan (Section 6) addresses all FAIR principles with appropriate detail. The decision to keep training data proprietary while open-sourcing code and evaluation benchmarks is well-justified.

Work Plan Table (Section 8) includes all required columns: task, duration, participants & effort, deliverables & due dates, technical descriptions, and computational resources. Task sequencing is logical with appropriate overlaps.
Month 7 report (Section 7) explicitly lists five specific content commitments aligned with FFplus questionnaire requirements.
The estimated training corpus size (4.2 GB) is credible for 50,000+ instruction-output pairs at the specified document lengths.
ALD's organisational capability is well-documented: 219 employees, 18 developers, proprietary ERP and SaaS platform, 6 years of production data.
ALD's AI/ML experience is now adequately evidenced: ML-based ERP analytics (anomaly detection, demand forecasting), GPT-4 API integration, and LoRA fine-tuning experiments on Llama-3.

Key staff profiles are well-specified with relevant experience: Danilov (coordination, 15 years construction), Kovalenko (software engineering, PyTorch, 10 years), Marchenko (data engineering, 7 years), Bondar (cost estimation domain expert, 12 years).
ZNU's laboratory profile includes quantified research output (25 publications, 8 in last year, Scopus-indexed), a funded research project (3.59M UAH), and international collaborations.

ZNU staff profiles are adequate but would benefit from citing specific publication titles rather than counts — Dr. Shylo's '12 publications in HPC and AI' and Dr. Petrov's '8 publications including neural architecture search' are promising but generic.
The complementarity matrix is excellent — clear delineation with no overlap, and ZNU's role is explicitly limited to engineering/technical activities.
Eligibility confirmations are comprehensive: non-FFplus beneficiary, non-Type-1 participation, Ukraine's Digital Europe association (with source), and SME status for both entities.

HPC resource justification (Section 1) provides a thorough bottom-up calculation: 1,800 hours/run × 3 runs + 600 hyperparameter search + 500 baselines + 500 evaluation + 1,000 contingency = 8,000 GPU-node-hours. This is credible and well-justified.
The EuroHPC access plan identifies specific target systems (LUMI, Leonardo, MareNostrum 5) and mentions National Competence Centre assistance — demonstrating awareness of the application process.
Cost breakdown table is clear and compliant: ALD at €185K (68.5%) and ZNU at €85K (31.5%), both within individual caps, total €270K within consortium maximum.
Personnel rates (€8,000/PM for ALD, €6,000/PM for ZNU) are reasonable for qualified engineering roles in the Ukrainian market.
Other Direct Costs are well-itemised: travel (€9K total across both partners), dataset acquisition (€5K), cloud computing for development (€10K), ERP infrastructure (€5K), HPC contingency (€15K).
No indirect costs, no sub-contracting, no common equipment — all compliance requirements met.
The commercial HPC contingency (€15K) is a prudent inclusion but may be insufficient for significant training; a brief contingency timeline would add clarity.

The prior review concern about the expert evaluation panel being potential sub-contracting is fully resolved — expert evaluation is conducted by ALD's own senior cost engineers (Vasyl Bondar and colleagues).

16 references covering: eligibility documentation [1], HPC fundamentals [2], domain literature [3], LLM limitations [4], PEFT methods [5, 6], societal context [7], structured data integration [8], distributed training [9], evaluation metrics [10], experiment tracking [11], trustworthy AI [12], model documentation [13], foundation models [14, 15], and memory optimization [16].
References are appropriately diverse: EU policy documents, peer-reviewed research, and technical documentation.
The Ukraine Digital Europe association reference [1] with URL is an important addition that addresses the prior eligibility concern.
All cited references appear to be real and accurately attributed.
